original_abstract: Electrochemical synthesis of ammonia via the nitrate reduction reaction (NO3RR) has been intensively researched as an alternative to the traditional Haber-Bosch process. Most research focuses on the low concentration range representative of the nitrate level in wastewater, leaving the high concentration range, which exists in nuclear and fertilizer wastes, unexplored. The use of a concentrated electrolyte (≥1 M) for higher rate production is hampered by poor hydrogen transfer kinetics. Herein, we demonstrate that a cocatalytic system of Ru/Cu2O catalyst enables NO3RR at 10.0 A in 1 M nitrate electrolyte in a 16 cm2 flow electrolyzer, with 100% faradaic efficiency toward ammonia. Detailed mechanistic studies by deuterium labeling and operando Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy allow us to probe the hydrogen transfer rate and intermediate species on Ru/Cu2O. Ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) simulations reveal that adsorbed hydroxide on Ru nanoparticles increases the density of the hydrogen-bonded water network near the Cu2O surface, which promotes the hydrogen transfer rate. Our work highlights the importance of engineering synergistic interactions in cocatalysts for addressing the kinetic bottleneck in electrosynthesis.
humanized_abstract: The nitrate reduction reaction (NO3RR) has been studied as a potential route for the electrochemical synthesis of ammonia using less energy than the traditional Haber-Bosch process. Most studies are carried out at low nitrate concentrations, that is in the range of wastewater concentrations, and there is little information at the high nitrate concentrations of nuclear wastes and fertilizer production wastes. The use of concentrated electrolytes, with concentrations ≥ 1 M, in order to increase the production rate is also limited by the slow hydrogen transport to the electrode. Here, we show that a cocatalyst system consisting of a Ru/Cu2O catalyst is capable of producing ammonia at 10.0 A in 1 M nitrate in a 16 cm2 flow electrolyzer with 100% faradaic efficiency. The influence of hydrogens transfer to NO3− was investigated using deuterium labeling experiments and operando Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy over the Ru/Cu2O catalyst. By using these experiments along with ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) simulations we are able to investigate the mechanism of NO3RR in more detail.

The use of concentrated electrolytes, with concentrations ≥ 1 M, in order to increase the production rate is also limited by the slow hydrogen transport to the electrode. Here, we show that a cocatalyst system consisting of a Ru/Cu2O catalyst is capable of producing ammonia at 10.0 A in 1 M nitrate in a 16 cm2 flow electrolyzer with 100% faradaic efficiency. By using these experiments along with ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) simulations we are able to investigate the mechanism of NO3RR in more detail.
